payload.text: realiza un app con las caracteristicas de la foto que te adjunte, esta app tiene como finalidad es llevar el control de pagos fiscales, parafiscales y servicios. que realice el calculo del presupuesto anual de pagos de cada servicio e impuestos. ademas que tenga la capacidad de notificar a las personas involucradas en realizar los pagos via whatsapp. Esta aplicacion tendra una base de datos almacenada en la nube (Google sheets), realiza el scripts para conecta y registrar los datos de dicha aplicacion, este scripts debe tener la capacidad de crear las hojas y la estructura de estas hojas segun los datos a registrar.

**Defining the Frontend Scope**

I'm now zeroing in on defining the scope of this React frontend, keeping the payments aspect central. The image reference helps set the stage, and I'm identifying the core functionalities. Forms are the foundation; I'm mapping them in my head to envision the structure.


**Refining the UI Components**

I'm now concretizing the UI components I'll need, like date pickers and text inputs, which correspond directly with the image's layout. I'm focusing on "Registro de Pagos y Servicios" and "Control Fiscal y Parafiscal". I also remember that calculating annual budget projections is a key feature and will integrate this. I'm also planning for WhatsApp integration, likely using a `mailto` or `wa.me` link. The essential point is using Google Sheets as the "backend" via Google Apps Script.


**Outlining the Tech Stack**

I'm solidifying the technical stack; React 18 with TypeScript and Tailwind CSS are confirmed for the frontend. I'll use `lucide-react` for icons and `recharts` for budget visualizations. The backend will be Google Apps Script, with the code provided for the user to deploy. I'm laying out the core files: `metadata.json`, `index.html`, `index.tsx`, `App.tsx`, `types.ts`, `components/PaymentForm.tsx`, and `components/Dashboard.tsx`.


**Detailing the Backend Integration**

I'm now detailing the Google Apps Script integration, specifically the backend code. I've decided to structure it as `backend/Code.gs` for clarity. I'll include `doPost` to handle data posting, `doGet` for optional data fetching, and `setupSheet` for sheet management. This provides a more robust and complete solution.


**Finalizing the Components**

I'm now putting the finishing touches on the essential UI elements. I've designed the `PaymentForm` component, modeling it off the image, and building out the inputs for date, organism, type, amount, etc., using Tailwind. I'm also implementing the WhatsApp integration with the pre-filled message, and I will handle state management in the `App.tsx` file by putting together the form and the dashboard. I plan to use `recharts` for the dashboard visualizations. I will also be mocking the API calls initially, as I do not yet have the API URL.


**Addressing CORS Issues**

I'm now focusing on a robust approach to handle cross-origin requests. I've realized that dealing with CORS can be tricky, so I'm opting to implement the backend service to explicitly accept the Web App URL, allowing for easier user configuration. I'm also drafting the core code structure, including the Tailwind CSS classes needed, and I am structuring my annual budget projection calculations.


**Integrating the App Structure**

I'm now integrating the core application structure, focusing on `components/PaymentForm.tsx`, `components/BudgetDashboard.tsx`, and `App.tsx`. The design's "blue header, white card" details are being implemented. A WhatsApp notification button is in place post-submission. The Google Apps Script code is nearly ready for the user, to be copied to script.google.com.

Here is the complete React application code, including the Google Apps Script required for the backend connection.

### Instructions for the Google Sheets Backend
To make the "Save to Cloud" feature work, you must:
1. Create a new Google Sheet.
2. Go to **Extensions > Apps Script**.
3. Copy the content of the file `backend/Code.js` (provided below) into the script editor.
4. Save and Click **Deploy > New Deployment**.
5. Select **Web app**.
6. Set **Who has access** to **Anyone**.
7. Click **Deploy** and copy the **Web App URL**.
8. Paste this URL into the application's settings icon (top right) or `constants.ts`.

React entry point
TypeScript definitions for the application
Global constants
Google Apps Script code for the backend
Service to communicate with Google Sheets
The main form component matching the image design
Visualization component for annual budget calculation
Main application container

payload.text:
Crea un sistema de gestión de usuarios y roles para la aplicación de control de pagos. Define al menos tres roles: Administrador (acceso total), Pagador (puede registrar y notificar pagos) y Visor (solo puede ver la información). Asigna permisos específicos a cada rol y asegúrate de que la base de datos en Google Sheets refleje estas restricciones de acceso.

Desarrolla una funcionalidad de historial de pagos detallado para la aplicación. Cada registro de pago debe incluir fecha, monto, tipo de pago (fiscal, parafiscal, servicio), descripción del pago, y el nombre o identificador de la persona o entidad a la que se realizó. Almacena esta información en Google Sheets y permite que sea consultada y filtrada fácilmente.

Diseña un dashboard interactivo para la aplicación de control de pagos. Debe incluir gráficos y métricas clave como el presupuesto anual de pagos fiscales, parafiscales y servicios, el total de pagos realizados, los pagos pendientes y un calendario de próximos vencimientos. Utiliza Google Sheets como fuente de datos y asegúrate de que el dashboard se actualice automáticamente.

**Initiating Project Analysis**

I'm currently breaking down the request to enhance "FiscalControl Pro." My focus is on the core goals, which involve adding user and role management to the existing app, including three specific roles: Admin, Payer, and Viewer. This initial analysis is laying the groundwork for more detailed feature planning.


**Defining Feature Permissions**

Now, I'm defining specific permissions for each user role. These permissions will influence data access and application functionality. The plan involves mapping these permissions to Google Sheets integrations. I'm focusing on "payer" and "viewer" roles, along with administrator level privileges. The goal is fine-grained control for all aspects.


**Outlining Backend Implementation**

I'm now outlining the backend implementation details. My focus has shifted towards the data retrieval side and integrating Google Sheets. I need to implement fetching data and develop a history view and calendar view within the UI. I will also develop simulated authentication with defined user roles and privileges.


**Refining API Actions**

I'm now updating `doPost` to manage different actions like registering or fetching payments. I'm focusing on implementing `getPayments` to read from the sheet. My self-correction is about implementing role-based permissions in the frontend, while the App Script handles the data store as a backend solution.

I'm now designing the user interface. I'm developing a `PaymentHistory.tsx` component and am implementing a table view with filtering. I'll also add a "Pending Payments" card and an "Upcoming Maturities" list to the dashboard. My final step is breaking down the implementation steps, starting by updating types: adding `User`, `Role`, and updating `PaymentRecord`, and then updating the backend (Google Apps Script).


**Implementing Data Retrieval**

I'm now focusing on reading payment history. I'm building a `fetchPayments` function. I've decided to handle data retrieval with `doGet` instead of `doPost`, for now. Next, I'll create a simple user/role selection component for login and then the History component with filter capabilities. After this, I'll update the Dashboard with calendar/pending logic, and add the "Description" field to the payment form. Finally, I will integrate login, routing, and new tabs in the main app.


**Revising Calendar Display**

I've decided a simple "Upcoming Deadlines" list is more appropriate than a full calendar for the dashboard. It will be sorted by date and easier to scan quickly. This aligns better with the fiscal control's needs.

I realized I can't restrict sheet access directly with code, so I'm reframing the "access restrictions" request. I'll focus on role management within the system, using a 'Users' sheet in the backend script, even if the demo uses frontend login.

I'm finalizing code changes: updating types with `User`, `Role`, adding `doGet` in the backend, handling a 'Users' sheet, and implementing `getPayments` and new components: `Login.tsx`, `PaymentHistory.tsx`.

To fulfill your request, I will implement a Role-Based Access Control (RBAC) system, a detailed history view with filtering, and an enhanced dashboard with upcoming deadlines.

Here is the specification of changes:

1.  **Backend (`backend/Code.js`)**:
    *   Update `doPost` to handle different actions ('create' vs 'read').
    *   Add functionality to read all records from the Sheet to populate the Dashboard and History.
    *   Add a "Users" sheet to store user roles (simulated for this implementation).

2.  **Types (`types.ts`)**:
    *   Add `User`, `Role` ('admin', 'payer', 'viewer'), and updated `PaymentRecord` interfaces (adding `description`).

3.  **Services (`services/sheetService.ts`)**:
    *   Add `fetchPayments` to retrieve data from the cloud.

4.  **Components**:
    *   **New `Login.tsx`**: A simulated login screen to select roles (Admin, Pagador, Visor).
    *   **New `PaymentHistory.tsx`**: A tabular view of payments with filters for text, type, and status.
    *   **Update `PaymentForm.tsx`**: Add the `description` field.
    *   **Update `Dashboard.tsx`**: Add "Pending Payments" metrics and a "Calendar/Upcoming Maturities" list.

5.  **Main `App.tsx`**:
    *   Manage User state.
    *   Implement permission logic to show/hide tabs based on the user's role.

Here is the implementation:

entries.description: Added User, Role types and updated PaymentRecord
Updated initial form state with description
Updated backend to handle reading data and user simulation
Added fetchPayments and updated structure for actions
New component for user authentication
Added Description field
New component for detailed history and filtering
Added Pending Payments card and Calendar view
Integrated Login and Permission logic

payload.text: Add a confirmation step or toast notification upon successful saving of a payment record in PaymentForm.

**Adding Confirmation Step**

I'm focusing on enhancing the user experience post-payment record saving in `PaymentForm.tsx`. The current use of `alert()` is rudimentary. I'm considering a confirmation step, maybe a toast notification, for a more elegant and less disruptive confirmation.


**Refining User Confirmation**

I'm working on replacing the basic `alert()` calls in `handleSubmit` within `PaymentForm.tsx`. I've decided on a toast notification approach for a more user-friendly confirmation. I'll introduce `showToast` and `toastMessage` state variables, coupled with a simple custom toast UI element. This will replace the alert and auto-hide after a short delay, with visual style taken from the existing Tailwind CSS usage. My next step involves the code updates to the `PaymentForm.tsx` file itself.


**Implementing Notification UI**

I've added a `notification` state to `PaymentForm.tsx`, which uses a type to track a message and type (success/error). I'm replacing all `alert` calls with `showNotification`, which sets this state. I also added a timeout for a self-clearing notification. I will now render the notification based on this state. I've also implemented icons for success and error. I chose to use a fixed position toast at the bottom right.


**Adjusting Notification Styles**

I'm reassessing the notification's appearance in `PaymentForm.tsx`, specifically the animation. I've realized `animate-bounce-in` might not be a standard Tailwind class, so I'm switching to a more widely supported approach, possibly `transition` or `animate-fade-in` if it's available. I'll stick to established utility classes to ensure broad compatibility. I will also add `CheckCircle` and `X` from `lucide-react` import.


**Finalizing Alert Replacements**

I am now completing the full replacement of the outdated `alert()` calls within `PaymentForm.tsx`. I've integrated the `showNotification` function, and the related JSX for displaying the toast notification. I am also updating the conditional to call `showNotification` with the appropriate success or error message, leveraging the state for a smooth user experience. My next step will be to test and verify the notification's appearance and behavior.


**Testing Notification Implementation**

I'm now focusing on rigorously testing the toast notification system in `PaymentForm.tsx`. I'll confirm that success and error messages are correctly displayed, with proper styling and duration. Furthermore, I'll pay attention to the user experience.


**Refining WhatsApp Integration**

I'm now integrating WhatsApp notification functionality within `PaymentForm.tsx`, building upon the toast notification system. I've added error handling to prevent initiating a WhatsApp message before a payment is recorded. I'm constructing a formatted message that includes details like the organism, description, payment type, and amount, using template literals and string interpolation. I am crafting a WhatsApp-compatible URL for easy sharing.


**Integrating WhatsApp Sharing**

I'm now focusing on the WhatsApp integration within `PaymentForm.tsx`, adding the logic for a `WhatsApp` button. I'm building a function to construct the formatted WhatsApp message using data from the form. I'm also preparing a URL for sharing the pre-filled message, which I'll integrate with the button. I'll make sure error handling is in place to prevent WhatsApp attempts before form submission.


**Adding Form Fields**

I'm now adding form fields to gather essential payment details. I'm focusing on "dateRegistered" and "organism". I'm implementing input fields to capture these details within `PaymentForm.tsx`, ensuring proper data handling and validation for a smoother user flow.


**Adding Financial Details**

I'm now integrating financial input fields within `PaymentForm.tsx`, specifically for payment type and amount, to gather crucial data. I'm leveraging Tailwind CSS classes for consistent styling and a user-friendly interface. These additions enhance data capture for more complete payment records, streamlining the payment process.


**Adding Payment Details**

I'm now integrating payment and date fields into `PaymentForm.tsx`. I've added a number input for the payment amount with proper formatting and validation. I've also incorporated a date input for the real payment date, ensuring these fields enhance data capture and streamlined user experience.



**Refining UI Considerations**

I'm now integrating form fields to collect data around the unidad tributaria. I've designed three fields, "unitCode", "unitTaxRate", and "unitAddress", with specific input types. I'm focusing on ensuring data consistency and a user-friendly experience by leveraging consistent styling and validation, all within the `PaymentForm.tsx` context. I'm finalizing the form structure.


**Adding Unit Details**

I'm now integrating form fields to collect "unitName" and "municipality" details. I'm focusing on text input fields within `PaymentForm.tsx`, ensuring proper data capture and a user-friendly experience by leveraging Tailwind CSS classes for consistent styling. I'm finalizing the form structure, ensuring all data points are present.


**Updating Button Actions**

I'm now making final updates to the `PaymentForm.tsx` buttons. I've focused on enhancing the user flow. I'm leveraging Tailwind classes for consistent styling. My main focus is on the action button interactions for a more streamlined experience, with focus on the "WhatsApp" notification aspect.

To add a proper confirmation notification (Toast) when a payment is saved, I'll update `components/PaymentForm.tsx`. I'll introduce a `notification` state to handle the message visibility and style, replacing the browser's native `alert()` with a modern UI component that appears at the bottom-right of the screen.

entries.description: Replaced native alerts with a custom Toast notification for successful record saving

payload.text: la logica de notificacion por via whatsapp sera automatica es decir que antes de cumplir la fecha de vencimiento de pago (3 dias antes) se envia de forma atomatica la notificacion (mensaje).

**Defining the WhatsApp Flow**

I'm now zeroing in on defining the optimal WhatsApp notification flow. The goal is automation 3 days prior to the payment due date. Right now, there is a manual WhatsApp notification option that I must replace, streamlining the process. This automated approach is the core of what needs to be implemented.


**Analyzing Automation Challenges**

I've hit a snag. The user desires *automatic* WhatsApp notifications, but my preliminary analysis reveals a critical constraint. `wa.me` links, the user's likely preference given the current setup, *cannot* be automated server-side. They require user interaction, so I must find an alternative, perhaps shifting the paradigm if possible. Right now, I am exploring options for a solution.


**Exploring API Solutions**

I am now focusing on the practical application of a WhatsApp notification via an external service. I have determined that while the logic for due date checks will reside in Google Apps Script, the actual WhatsApp message sending will require an external API due to Apps Script limitations. I've considered several options, like Twilio, the Meta Graph API, or a simpler bridge service. Given the user's preference and the context, I must now address the complexity of setting up and verifying the Meta Graph API, as well as associated costs.


**Designing the GAS Implementation**

I'm now detailing the implementation within Google Apps Script (`backend/Code.js`). My plan is to include a time-driven trigger. This trigger, which runs daily, will scan the sheet for upcoming payments and send notifications three days before the due date. I cannot change the app to sign up for a WhatsApp API. I'll implement the trigger and a placeholder `sendWhatsAppMessage` function, clearly stating the user needs to add their API details. The first Function will check upcoming payments, and the second function will eventually send WhatsApp messages.


**Refining the API Call**

I'm now zeroing in on the `sendWhatsAppMessage` function. It needs to handle the API call. Since I lack API keys, I'll write a robust skeleton using `UrlFetchApp` they can customize, even if just to send the WhatsApp message or use a `console.log` / email as a fallback. I'll need to instruct the user on how to set up the time-driven trigger manually. I can provide them a `setupTrigger()` function to run. I'm assessing the changes needed, such as adding a phone number field to the `PaymentForm`.


**Implementing Phone Field**

I'm currently focused on incorporating the `contactPhone` field across the application. I'm adding it to `types.ts` and `constants.ts` to update the data models, and then adding an input field in the `PaymentForm.tsx`. In the backend (`backend/Code.js`), I'll modify `doPost` to save the new phone number, add the functions `checkUpcomingPayments`, and add a `sendWhatsAppNotification` stub, containing instructions for the user to configure an API such as CallMeBot/Twilio. I will also add the `installTrigger` function. I'm also carefully noting existing column indices for later usage.


**Adding Phone Field Integration**

I am now focusing on integrating the phone field throughout the application and implementing the related WhatsApp logic. The `types.ts` and `constants.ts` files have been updated to include the `contactPhone` field. The input field in `PaymentForm.tsx` is also added. Finally, in `backend/Code.js`, I'm modifying `doPost` to save the new phone number and adding the `checkDailyNotifications` function. I will add the logic to find the rows, then implement the attempt at an API call. For testing, I'll fallback to `MailApp.sendEmail` if the WhatsApp URL is empty.

Para implementar notificaciones automáticas vía WhatsApp 3 días antes del vencimiento, necesitamos realizar cambios tanto en el **Frontend** (para capturar el número de teléfono) como en el **Backend (Google Apps Script)** (para ejecutar la verificación diaria automática).

**Nota Importante:** Google Apps Script no puede abrir "WhatsApp Web" automáticamente. Para enviar mensajes automáticos desde el servidor, se requiere un servicio de pasarela (Gateway) como **Twilio**, **Meta Business API**, o servicios sencillos como **CallMeBot**.

En el código del backend (`backend/Code.js`), he incluido la lógica completa para buscar los pagos próximos a vencer y una función preparada para conectarse a una API de WhatsApp.

Aquí están los cambios necesarios:

1.  **`types.ts`**: Agregar el campo `contactPhone`.
2.  **`constants.ts`**: Inicializar el campo de teléfono.
3.  **`components/PaymentForm.tsx`**: Añadir el campo de entrada para el teléfono.
4.  **`backend/Code.js`**:
    *   Actualizar la base de datos para guardar el teléfono.
    *   Crear la función `checkUpcomingPayments` (el script automático).
    *   Crear la función `createTrigger` para que el script se ejecute solo cada día.

entries.description: Added contactPhone to PaymentRecord
Added contactPhone to initial state
Added Phone Number input field
Added Logic for Automatic Notifications and Trigger Setup

payload.text:
agreguemos la logica de aprobacion de pagos mediante 2 pasos:
1.- el Pagador registra y notifica mediante un reporte que envia al Auditor.

2.- el auditor despues de hacer la revision de los pagos, lo aprueba o no. Realizando un reporte de pagos aprobados o no.

**Defining the Approval Process**

I'm now zeroing in on the core goal: implementing a two-step approval system for payments. The payer, or "Pagador," initiates a payment, and it begins in a "Pending Review" state. I'm focusing on clarifying the exact states and transitions involved to map out the logic, with the intention of clarifying the steps.


**Clarifying Workflow Steps**

I've outlined the critical flow. The Payer submits, triggering "Pending Review." The Auditor, with an admin view, either Approves or Rejects the payment. I'm focusing on incorporating notifications/reports for these Auditor actions, and determining the appropriate context for that Auditor role, and thinking of roles like "Viewer" or "Admin" within the existing system. I'm also ensuring the current system can handle the new states.


**Implementing Update Functionality**

I'm now focusing on the implementation details for updates. I've realized I need a new action, `updateStatus`, or a more generic `update`, which will be crucial for Auditors to modify payment statuses. The `doPost` function currently handles `create` and `read`; I need to add update functionality. On the frontend, I'll add `updatePaymentStatus` to `sheetService.ts`. I also need to modify UI components: the `PaymentForm` will now default payments to 'Pending Review' (with a "Report sent" simulation). In `PaymentHistory.tsx`, I'll introduce "Approve" and "Reject" buttons, visible only to auditor roles.


**Updating Roles and Reporting**

I'm now integrating the "Auditor Visor" role from `Login.tsx`, meaning the "Viewer" role, and Admin, should have approval rights. I'm focusing on "Notify Auditor" button (WhatsApp) as the first step for all payments, triggering "Pending Review." I'm then defining the report system: Upon approval or rejection, a "Report" (e.g., WhatsApp notification) is sent to either the payer or group, triggered by Auditor actions.
\n\n
I'm drafting the code changes. I will update `types.ts` to include 'Pending Review', 'Approved', 'Rejected' statuses. The backend in `Code.js` requires added `update` logic to change the status column by ID. I need to add `updatePaymentStatus` to `sheetService.ts`. I'm now drafting the implementation of the workflow's specific steps, with the payer action, and the auditor action.


**Refining Update Actions**

I'm now integrating the UI for payments, focusing on the `PaymentHistory.tsx` component. I will add an Action column, with buttons for Auditors to Approve or Reject, based on the current user. I'll pass the `currentUser` prop to check roles. I need to make the API call to update the status, and ensure the generation of the "Report" WhatsApp link. In `App.tsx`, I'll pass the user to `PaymentHistory`. I will also incorporate Google Apps Script `LockService` in the backend to ensure data integrity during updates and handle potential race conditions. The update logic will iterate through rows to find and update by ID.


**Implementing Approval Checks**

I'm now focusing on defining the permission checks, ensuring the Payer creates payments, and the Admin/Auditor approves them. I'm clarifying the steps, registering the payment and notifying, then reviewing and approving or rejecting. I'm also ensuring the message tweaks are correct. I'm finally putting the pieces together, and I will be focusing on verifying that all the parts are connected.

To implement the 2-step approval logic (Registration/Notification by Payer -> Approval/Rejection by Auditor), I need to update the data flow and user interfaces.

Here is the specification of changes:

1.  **Data Model (`types.ts`)**:
    *   Update `status` to support specific states: `'Pending Review'`, `'Approved'`, `'Rejected'`.

2.  **Backend (`backend/Code.js`)**:
    *   Add an `update` action to the `doPost` function. This will allow the Auditor to change the status of a specific payment record in the Google Sheet by searching for its ID.

3.  **Service (`services/sheetService.ts`)**:
    *   Add a `updatePaymentStatus` function to communicate with the backend.

4.  **Frontend - Payer View (`components/PaymentForm.tsx`)**:
    *   Set the default status of new records to `'Pending Review'`.
    *   Update the WhatsApp notification message to be framed as a "Report to Auditor" (e.g., "Solicitud de Revisión").

5.  **Frontend - Auditor View (`components/PaymentHistory.tsx`)**:
    *   Add an "Acciones" (Actions) column to the table.
    *   Check `user.role`: If the user is an **Admin** or **Viewer** (acting as Auditor), show "Approve" (Check) and "Reject" (X) buttons.
    *   Implement the logic: When approved/rejected, update the backend, update the local state, and trigger a WhatsApp "Reporte de Auditoría" notifying the result.

6.  **Integration (`App.tsx`)**:
    *   Pass the `currentUser` object to `PaymentHistory` so it knows which buttons to show.

Here is the implementation:

entries.description: Updated status type to include approval states
Added update action to handle status changes
Added updatePaymentStatus function
Updated status default and notification message for Auditor
Added Approval Actions for Auditor and Reports
Pass user and scriptUrl to PaymentHistory

diagnostics.message: This comparison appears to be unintentional because the types 'PaymentStatus' and '"Pending"' have no overlap.

payload.text:
**Analyzing PaymentStatus Error**

I'm currently focused on resolving a type mismatch error in `components/Dashboard.tsx` on line 42. It seems the comparison between `PaymentStatus` and the string literal '"Pending"' is the issue. I'm investigating how `PaymentStatus` is defined within `types.ts` to understand the root cause.


**Correcting Payment Status**

I've pinpointed the root cause: a type mismatch in `components/Dashboard.tsx` line 42. My analysis shows the issue stems from comparing the `PaymentStatus` enum (specifically, 'Pending Review', 'Approved', or 'Rejected') with the string literal "'Pending'". To resolve this, I must replace the string literal with the correct `PaymentStatus` value, 'Pending Review', to align with the type definition. I confirmed this value's correct usage via cross-referencing in `PaymentForm.tsx`.


**Confirming String Value**

I've validated that 'Pending Review' is indeed the intended string value for the `status` field. I will now adjust `components/Dashboard.tsx` to utilize this string value within the filter logic. I am preparing the XML output.


**Calculating Budget Summaries**

I'm now focusing on calculating budget summaries within the `Dashboard` component. I've implemented a `useMemo` hook to efficiently process `records`. It aggregates data by `paymentType` to track total spent, count, and project annual amounts.  I have also accounted for a total amount spent, calculated from all the records. I am working on the pending logic next.


**Implementing Pending Calculations**

I am now implementing the pending calculations for the dashboard. I've filtered `records` to find pending review items and then calculated the `pendingAmount`. I've also incorporated logic to display upcoming deadlines, which are sorted by the payment date within a 30-day timeframe and limited to the top 5. I will now integrate this into the dashboard.


**Displaying Budget Totals**

I'm now integrating the calculated budget summaries into the `Dashboard`. I'm focusing on displaying the total spent, record count, pending amount, and annual projection. I've used `toLocaleString()` to format the numbers for better readability. I've added a container that renders the data in a visually appealing grid format with relevant styling.


**Visualizing Budget Data**

I'm now focusing on the visual representation of budget data within the dashboard. I've integrated a bar chart using `Recharts`, displaying 'Gasto Actual' and 'Proyección Anual' for each `paymentType`. The chart renders within a `ResponsiveContainer` and includes a `Tooltip` for formatted dollar values. I am formatting the chart data now.


**Rendering Upcoming Deadlines**

I am now focusing on rendering the upcoming deadlines section on the dashboard. I'm dynamically mapping the `upcomingDeadlines` array to generate individual deadline entries. Each entry showcases the organism, payment type, payment date, and amount. I'm using a `Calendar` icon and applying styling for a clean, informative presentation.


**Displaying Budget Pie Chart**

I'm now integrating a pie chart to visualize the distribution of expenses within the dashboard. I've populated the chart with the `budgetData`, mapping each expense type to a section. I'm focusing on rendering a clean, responsive chart within the `ResponsiveContainer`. I'm formatting the `PieChart` to display percentages and utilizing color coding for each expense category for a clear visual.

entries.description: Fix type mismatch by updating the status comparison to use 'Pending Review' instead of 'Pending' which does not exist in PaymentStatus type.
